title: द्वितीयोनुवाकः
pada: नमो॒ । रोहि॑ताय । स्थ॒-पत॑ये । वृ॒क्षाणाम् । पत॑ये । नमो॒ ॥
krama: नमो॒ रोहि॑ताय । रोहि॑ताय स्थ॒पत॑ये । स्थ॒पत॑ये वृ॒क्षाणाम् । वृ॒क्षाणा॒-म्पत॑ये । पत॑ये॒ नमो॒ । नमो॒ इति॑ नमो॒ ॥

sayana.text:
'Rohitah' is the red-colored one. 'Sthapatih' is the Chief (Prabhu). Salutation be to that Rudra. Salutation to Rudra who is the protector of trees (vriksha). (9)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namo rohitaya'—to the red-colored one. Someone says: the syllable 'Ro' refers to the form of speech; he who is beneficial (hita) for that, or residing in that. 'Sthapataye'—to the cutter (khandaka). Or: the creator of houses, etc. Another says: he who stays (sthata) everywhere and is the protector (pata) of everything. Salutation to the Lord of trees (vriksha). [Trees are those which he establishes and cuts]. (9)

He speaks the tenth Yajus:
Salutation to the minister, to the merchant, salutation to the lord of thickets.

abhinava_shankara.text:
---
82                    Sri Rudra Bhashyam
---

[Mantra 9:]
Obeisance to Rohita (the red-colored one), to the Sthapati (the lord-who-stands), to the Lord of trees — obeisance.

Now the ninth [commentary]: — "Namo rohitaya" (Obeisance to Rohita). "Rohita" means the one of red (lohita) color; "sthapati" means the lord who both stands (sthata) and protects (pata) — [this is] a word of the prshodaradi class (a grammatical class of irregularly formed words). Obeisance to such a one who is the Lord and master of trees — this is the meaning. Here the word "sthapati" conveys the reason for His lordship over trees: it is fitting that He who dwells within trees and protects them should be their lord. The word "rohita" (red) befits the Supreme Lord as the presiding deity of trees, for it will be stated later: "They who in trees are yellow-green, blue-necked, and deep red." Thus ends the ninth.

[Mantra 10:]
Obeisance to the wise counsellor (mantrin), to the merchant (vanija), to the Lord of thickets — obeisance.

Now the tenth [commentary]: — "Mantrine" (to the wise counsellor). The dense growths of forest-shrubs and the like are called "kakshas" (thickets); obeisance to their Lord — this is the primary meaning. But [one might object]: How can the Lord exercise sovereignty over what is exceedingly dense and impenetrable? Addressing this doubt about the impenetrability [of thickets], the text specifies with the word "vanijaya" (to the merchant): A body of merchants is called "vanija"; just as a merchant comprehends and makes clear all the dealings of a country, so too this [Lord] comprehends all things — nothing is impenetrable to Him — such is the intent. Another reason for His lordship over thickets is given
abhinava_shankara.sanskrit:
---
८२                    श्रीरुद्रभाष्यम् ।
---

नमो रोहिताय स्थपतये वृक्षाणां पतये
नमः ॥

अथ नवमः— नमो रोहितायेति । लोहितवर्णो रो-
हितः, स्थाता चाक्षो पाता चेति स्थपतिः, पृषोदरादिः;
एवंभूताय वृक्षाणां पतये स्वामिने नमः इत्यर्थः । अत्र
स्थपतिशब्देन वृक्षस्वामित्वे हेतुरुच्यते, वृक्षेषु स्थित्वा
वृक्षान्पालयतस्तत्स्वामित्वौचित्यात् । रोहितशब्देन वृक्षाधि-
ष्ठातुः परमेश्वरस्य स्वरूपयुक्तं भवति, 'ये वृक्षेषु सस्पिञ्जरा
नीलाग्रीवा विलोहिताः' इति हि वक्ष्यते । इति नवमः ॥

नमो मन्त्रिणे वाणिजाय कक्षाणां प-
तये नमः ॥

अथ दशमः— मन्त्रिण इति । वनगुल्मादयोऽतिगहन-
पदार्थाः कक्षाः तेषां स्वामी कक्षाणां पतिः, तस्मै नमः इति
प्रथममन्वयः; नतु अतिगहनेषु कथमीश्वरस्याधिपत्यं स्या-
त्? दुरवगाह्त्वादित्याशङ्कय विशिनष्टि— वाणिजायेति।
वणिजां समूहो वाणिजः, यथा वणिक् सर्वजनपद्व्यवहारा-
न्परिच्छिद्य विशदीकरोति, तथा अयमपि सकलवस्तुजाव-
मिति, नास्य किञ्चिदुरवगाहमस्तीति भावः । केषुवि-
